components.label: Web/Mobile Client
components.description: Requests home timeline via REST API; renders tweet_id list and fetches tweet content from a separate tweet store

components.label: Timeline Query Service + Fan-out Service
components.description: Two separate services: Fan-out Service writes to follower caches at tweet time; Timeline Query Service reads from cache and merges celebrity tweets at read time

components.label: Tweet Store + Social Graph DB
components.description: Tweet Store holds full tweet objects (content, media URLs, metadata); Social Graph DB holds follower/following relationships used by the fan-out service

components.label: Timeline Cache (Redis)
components.description: Stores pre-computed ordered tweet_id lists per user — NOT full tweet objects; key: user:{id}:timeline → sorted set of tweet_ids by timestamp

components.label: Fan-out Queue (Kafka)
components.description: Fan-out Service publishes a fan-out job per tweet; worker pool reads follower lists from Social Graph DB and writes tweet_id to each follower's timeline cache

connections.label: Read tweet_id list from timeline cache
Fetch full tweet objects by IDs + merge celebrity feed
Publish fan-out job on new tweet
Fan-out workers write tweet_id to follower caches
Read follower list from Social Graph DB

keyInsights: Timeline cache stores tweet_id ordered sets — NOT tweet objects — so fan-out is just appending an integer to N sorted sets; tweet content (text, media) is fetched separately and can be independently cached or updated

Hybrid fan-out threshold (~1M followers): below the threshold, push tweet_id to all follower caches at write time; above the threshold, skip fan-out and merge the celebrity's latest tweets at read time by fetching their personal feed
CQRS in practice: the Fan-out Service (write model) and Timeline Query Service (read model) share no code, no database tables, and no in-process state — they scale independently

Cache invalidation on delete: tweet deletion must remove the tweet_id from all timeline caches that contain it — this is handled by publishing a delete-fan-out job to the same queue; the Timeline Query Service also filters deleted tweet_ids before returning results

'While you were away' is a separate batch pipeline: it runs every few hours on users with no recent activity, computes a ranked summary of top missed tweets, and prepends it as a synthetic feed item — it never blocks the real-time read path

tradeoffs.decision: Fan-out on write (push) for regular users
tradeoffs.pro: O(1) read latency — timeline load is a single cache lookup; no computation at read time
tradeoffs.con: Write amplification proportional to follower count; a viral tweet from a user with 999K followers triggers 999K cache writes

tradeoffs.decision: Fan-out on read (pull) for celebrity accounts
tradeoffs.pro: Zero write amplification at tweet time — one DB write regardless of follower count
tradeoffs.con: Read-time merge cost proportional to number of celebrities a user follows; must be bounded (e.g. top 20 celebrities merged per read)

tradeoffs.decision: Storing tweet_ids in sorted sets vs full tweet objects
tradeoffs.pro: Fan-out write is O(1) per follower; tweet edits/deletes only require one object update, not N cache invalidations
tradeoffs.con: Every timeline read requires a second DB/cache lookup to hydrate tweet content; adds one network round-trip

antiPatterns: Storing full tweet objects in the timeline cache — a tweet edit or delete would require invalidating millions of cache entries across all follower timelines
Applying the same fan-out strategy to all users regardless of follower count — celebrity fan-out at 50M followers takes minutes and causes cache write storms
Querying the Social Graph DB synchronously on every tweet to find followers — at 6K tweets/sec this generates 6K expensive graph traversals per second; fan-out must be async via a queue
Using LIMIT/OFFSET for timeline pagination — timeline items are scored by timestamp, not row number; use cursor-based pagination with the last seen tweet_id as the cursor
